questions.question: Reference the previous question. After landing you have 10,300 LBS of fuel and your CG shifted from 24% MAC to 28% MAC. The MAC is 162 inches and it starts at 42 feet. What is the position (in %MAC) for your fuel tanks?
questions.context: Referenced question 23: You have 46,300 lbs of Jet A on board an aircraft weighing in at 197,800 LBS. How many liters do you have? (Assume 1 gallon = 6.75 LBS)

questions.question: Reference the previous question. How far from the reference datum is your landing CG?
questions.context: Referenced question 23: You have 46,300 lbs of Jet A on board an aircraft weighing in at 197,800 LBS. How many liters do you have? (Assume 1 gallon = 6.75 LBS) [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 24: Reference the previous question. After landing you have 10,300 LBS of fuel and your CG shifted from 24% MAC to 28% MAC. The MAC is 162 inches and it starts at 42 feet. What is the position (in %MAC) for your fuel tanks?

questions.question: Kona, a 60-lb Golden Retriever, is currently in the back seat (70% MAC) and jumps up front (12% MAC) for a better view in your 1900-lb airplane (MAC of 50%). What is the change in CG in terms of MAC?
Currently your CG is at 499.37 cm, your leading edge MAC is 413.9 cm and trailing edge MAC is 698.8 cm. What is the CG in percent MAC?

Referring to the previous questions: The CG of the KC-135 before the fuel transfer was 23% MAC and the fuel transferred to the B-52 shifts the CG to 27% MAC. The pilot moves fuel from a tank located at 77% MAC to a tank at -7% MAC to move the CG back to 23% MAC. How much fuel was moved between the tanks (do not take into account the fuel burned by the KC-135 engines during this time)?
questions.context: Referenced question 13: A KC-135 Stratotanker weighing 268,000 lbs is transferring JP-8 (specific gravity of 6.5 lbs/gal) to a B-52 at a rate of 1,763 L/min. Each of the KC-135's four engines burns 3,200 lb/h. If the B-52 needs to take on 50,000 lbs of gas, how much time does the fuel transfer take? [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 14: Referring to the previous question: Including the fuel transfer and the fuel burn from the KC-135's engines, how much fuel drains from the KC-135's fuel tanks during the process?

questions.question: From the above question what is your percent MAC after the shift if your LEMAC is 23.3 and your TEMAC is 62.7?
questions.context: Referenced question 5: After you shifted 143 lbs. of your 3460 lb. airplane, your CG shifted from 47.6" to 46.7". From what station did you shift the weight if it is now at station 45.4"?

questions.question: You are flying in your Piper Seminole, and you burn 80 gallons of avgas at an arm of 95" since the start of your trip. Your takeoff weight was 3739 lbs and CG was 89.2", which is 43% aft of your LEMAC of 73". What is your new CG in percent MAC?

The approved CG range for your aircraft is from 22.5% Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC) to 34.0% MAC. If the leading edge of MAC is located at station 100" (equivalent to 0% MAC) and the trailing edge is located at station 140" (100% MAC), what station is the leading edge of the approved MAC envelope?
